analogy: Think of "Challenges of Nation Building" through the chapter's own opening images: In this chapter… The first few years in the life of independent India were full of challenges. Some of the most pressing ones concerned national unity and territorial integrity of India. We begin the story of politics in India since Independence by looking at how... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 15 August 1947, 4 Politics in India since Independence, 14 August 1947,, 15 August 1947. But. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Challenges of Nation Building is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that in this chapter… The first few years in the life of independent India were full of challenges.

The text explains that some of the most pressing ones concerned national unity and territorial integrity of India.

The text explains that we begin the story of politics in India since Independence by looking at how three of these challenges of nation- building were successfully negotiated in the first decade after 1947. • Freedom came with partition, which resulted in large scale violence and displacement and challenged the very idea of a secular India. • The integration of the princely states into the Indian union needed urgent resolution. • The internal boundaries of the country needed to be drawn afresh to meet the aspirations of the people who spoke different languages.

The text explains that in the next two chapters we shall turn to other kinds of challenges faced by the country in this early phase.

The text explains that credit: Sunil Janah In a moment of optimism, Hindus and Hindus and Muslims in Kolkata in 1947 marked the end of communal violence by jointly flying the flags of India and Pakistan from trucks patrolling the city.

The text explains that this rare photograph captured the joy of freedom and the tragedy of partition in India and in Pakistan.

The text explains that challenges for the new nation-state At the hour of midnight on 14-15 August 1947, India attained independence.

The text explains that jawaharlal Nehru, the first prime minister of free India, addressed a special session of the Constituent Assembly that night.

The text explains that this was the famous ‘tryst with destiny’ speech that you are familiar with.

The text explains that this was the moment Indians had been waiting for.

The text explains that you have read in your history textbooks that there were many voices in our national movement.

The text explains that but there were two goals almost everyone agreed upon: one, that after Independence, we shall run our country through democratic government; and two, that the government will be run for the good of all, particularly the poor and the socially disadvantaged groups.

The text explains that now that the country was independent, the time had come to realise the promise of freedom.

The text explains that india was born as a nation-state in very difficult circumstances. perhaps no other country by then was born in a situation more difficult than that of India in 1947.

The text explains that freedom came with the partition of the country.

The text explains that the year 1947 was a year of unprecedented violence and trauma of displacement.

The text explains that credit: pIB prime minister Jawahar Lal Nehru speaking from the red Fort, 15 August 1947 1chapter challenges of nation Building

The text explains that the first and the immediate challenge was to shape a nation that was united, yet accommodative of the diversity in our society.

The text explains that there were serious questions about the future of India: Would India survive as a unified country?

The text explains that and there was an urgent question: How was integration of the territory of India to be achieved?

The text explains that you have already studied the Indian Constitution.

The text explains that you know that the Constitution granted fundamental rights and extended the right to vote to every citizen.

The text explains that india adopted representative democracy based on the parliamentary form of government.

The text explains that these features ensure that the political competition would take place in a democratic framework.

The text explains that challenges of Nation Building 5 A democratic constitution is necessary but not sufficient for establishing a democracy.

The text explains that the challenge was to develop democratic practices in accordance with the Constitution.

The text explains that the third challenge was to ensure the development and well- being of the entire society and not only of some sections.

The text explains that here again the Constitution clearly laid down the principle of equality and special protection to socially disadvantaged groups and religious and cultural communities.

The text explains that the Constitution also set out in the Directive Principles of State Policy the welfare goals that democratic politics must achieve.

The text explains that to what extent did India succeed in achieving the various objectives set out by the Constitution?

The text explains that this entire book is an attempt to respond to these questions.

The text explains that the book tells the story of politics in India since Independence so as to equip you to develop your own answers to big questions like these.

The text explains that in the first three chapters we look at how the three challenges mentioned above were faced in the early years after Independence.

The text explains that in this chapter, we focus on the first challenge of nation- building that occupied centre-stage in the years immediately after Independence.

The text explains that we shall then see how India chose to shape itself into a nation, united by a shared history and common destiny.

The text explains that in the next two chapters we shall turn to the challenge of establishing a democracy and achieving economic development with equality and justice.

The text explains that 6 Politics in India since Independence The Dawn of Freedom Faiz Ahmed Faiz This scarred, marred brightness, this bitten-by-night dawn - The one that was awaited, surely, this is not that dawn.

The text explains that somewhere, at least, must be a shore for the languid waves of the night, Somewhere at least must anchor the sad boat of the heart … Translation of an extract from urdu poem Subh-e-azadi We should begin to work in that spirit and in course of time all these angularities of the majority and minority communities, the Hindu community and the Muslim community – because even as regards Muslims you have pathans, punjabis, Shias, Sunnis and so on and among the Hindus you have Brahmins, Vaishnavas, Khatris, also Bengalees, Madrasis, and so on – will vanish. … You are free; you are free to go to your temples, you are free to go to your mosques or to any other place of worship in this State of pakistan.

The text explains that we must give them security and the rights of citizens in a democratic State.

The text explains that the drawing of the border demarcating the territory of each country marked the culmination of political developments that you have read about in the history textbooks.

The text explains that process of Partition Thus it was decided that what was till then known as ‘India’ would be divided into two countries, ‘India’ and ‘Pakistan’.

The text explains that consequences of partition The year 1947 was the year of one of the largest, most abrupt, unplanned and tragic transfer of population that human history has known.

The text explains that that is why we have not been able to entertain you lavishly – the way we wanted to.’ Source: English translation of Urdu short story Kasre-Nafsi Gandhi in Noakhali (now in Bangladesh) in 1947.

The text explains that the employees of the government and the railways were also ‘divided’.

The text explains that so, how would the government of India treat its Muslim citizens and other religious minorities (Sikhs, Christians, Jains, Buddhists, Parsis and Jews)?

The text explains that a decisive moment comes when Salim witnesses a students’ procession demanding fair treatment from the government.

The text explains that he was unhappy with what he saw as the Indian government’s decision not to honour its financial commitments to pakistan.

The text explains that the Government of India agreed to give pakistan its dues.

The text explains that it is said that the nation is to a large extent an “ imagined community” held together by common beliefs, history, political aspirations and imaginations.

The text explains that identify the features that make India a nation.

The text explains that read the following passage and answer the questions below: “In the history of nation-building only the Soviet experiment bears comparison with the Indian.

The text explains that there too, a sense of unity had to be forged between many diverse ethnic groups, religious, linguistic communities and social classes.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Politics in India since Independence, Chapter 1: Challenges of Nation Building

prelims_traps: Do not treat 15 August 1947 and 4 Politics in India since Independence as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Challenges of Nation Building; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Challenges of Nation Building through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 15 August 1947 and 4 Politics in India since Independence to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

Its people spoke different languages and followed different cultures and religions.
